_comment: Chronos Earth — story tours. Each step moves the timeline (year = signed calendar year, negative = BCE; or ma = millions of years ago for deep time), flies the camera to lon/lat/altitude, and shows (or speaks) the text. battleId optionally opens that battle's panel. Add a tour by copying one.

tours.title: The Rise and Fall of Rome
tours.description: From a village on the Tiber to a continental empire — and the long fall.

steps.title: A village on seven hills
steps.text: Legend says Rome was founded in 753 BCE by Romulus, on seven hills beside the river Tiber. For centuries it was just one small town among many in Italy.

steps.title: The Republic is born
steps.text: In 509 BCE the Romans threw out their last king and invented a republic — power shared between elected consuls, a senate, and the people. Its motto: S P Q R, the Senate and People of Rome.

steps.title: Disaster at Cannae
steps.text: Hannibal of Carthage crossed the Alps with elephants and, at Cannae in 216 BCE, destroyed a Roman army of eighty thousand in a single afternoon. Almost any other state would have surrendered. Rome refused — and slowly won the war instead.

steps.title: Caesar conquers Gaul
steps.text: Julius Caesar spent eight years conquering Gaul — modern France. At Alesia in 52 BCE he besieged the Gallic leader Vercingetorix, building two rings of walls — one to keep him in, one to keep his rescuers out. Victory made Caesar dangerously powerful.

steps.title: Actium: Republic becomes Empire
steps.text: After Caesar's murder, his heir Octavian fought Mark Antony and Cleopatra for everything. The naval battle of Actium in 31 BCE settled it. Octavian became Augustus, the first emperor — the Republic was over.

steps.title: The empire at its greatest
steps.text: In 117 CE, under the emperor Trajan, Rome ruled from Britain to the Persian Gulf — roads, aqueducts, cities and law across three continents. Watch the borders on the globe: this is as big as it ever got.

steps.title: Warning at Adrianople
steps.text: In 378 CE, Gothic refugees-turned-rebels annihilated a Roman army and killed the emperor Valens at Adrianople. The legions were no longer invincible, and the frontiers were cracking.

steps.title: The fall of the West
steps.text: In 476 CE the last western emperor, a boy called Romulus Augustulus, was quietly deposed. The western empire dissolved into new kingdoms — but Roman law, language and roads shaped Europe forever. The eastern half, Byzantium, lived on for another thousand years.

tours.title: Napoleon's Wars
tours.description: A young general seizes France, conquers Europe, and loses it all.

steps.title: From chaos, a general
steps.text: France's revolution had descended into chaos and war with all of Europe. In 1799 a brilliant young general from Corsica, Napoleon Bonaparte, seized power in Paris. Within five years he crowned himself emperor.

steps.title: Austerlitz — the masterpiece
steps.text: At Austerlitz in 1805, Napoleon deliberately weakened his own right flank to lure the Russians and Austrians off the high ground — then smashed through their centre. It is still studied as the perfect battle.

steps.title: The fatal march on Moscow
steps.text: In 1812 Napoleon marched six hundred thousand men into Russia. The Russians traded space for time, burned the land behind them, and fought him bloodily at Borodino. Napoleon took Moscow — and then winter took his army. Watch the front line on the globe sweep east and collapse back west.

steps.title: The Battle of the Nations
steps.text: Europe rose against the weakened emperor. At Leipzig in 1813 — the biggest battle the world had yet seen, half a million men — Napoleon was beaten and driven back to France.

steps.title: Waterloo
steps.text: Exiled, escaped, and emperor again for one hundred days, Napoleon met Wellington and Blücher near a Belgian village called Waterloo on the 18th of June 1815. By nightfall his last army was broken — and so was he.

steps.title: The rock in the Atlantic
steps.text: The British shipped Napoleon to Saint Helena, a tiny island three thousand kilometres from anywhere. He died there in 1821, aged 51 — leaving behind a legal code, a redrawn Europe, and an argument historians are still having.

tours.title: The Age of Dinosaurs
tours.description: 180 million years of giants — from Pangea to the asteroid.

steps.title: One world: Pangea
steps.text: 210 million years ago every continent was joined into one — Pangea. The first dinosaurs, like Coelophysis and Plateosaurus, were spreading across it. Look: you could walk from New Mexico to Germany.

steps.title: Jurassic giants
steps.text: By 150 million years ago Pangea was tearing apart and the Atlantic was being born. On the Morrison floodplains, Brachiosaurus and Stegosaurus browsed while Allosaurus hunted — and in Europe, Archaeopteryx, the first bird, took wing.

steps.title: The last kings
steps.text: 67 million years ago the continents are starting to look familiar. In western North America, Tyrannosaurus rex hunts Triceratops, while bus-sized Mosasaurs rule the seas. The dinosaurs' world seems eternal.

steps.title: The day the sky fell
steps.text: 66 million years ago a ten-kilometre asteroid struck the sea at Chicxulub, in modern Mexico. Firestorms, darkness and a years-long winter followed. Three quarters of all species — and every dinosaur that couldn't fly — vanished. Notice the markers are gone.

steps.title: A colder, newer world
steps.text: Twenty thousand years ago, in the grip of the ice age, giants walked again: woolly mammoths and woolly rhinos on the steppe, sabre-toothed cats in the tar pits, giant sloths on the pampas. And this time, humans were watching them — and painting them on cave walls.
